paper_title: When More Sampling Hurts: The Modal Ceiling and Correlation Ceiling of Test-Time Scaling

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: People overthink; language models over-sample, and the extra effort can talk both into a worse answer. Reasoning systems answer a hard question by sampling it many times (test-time scaling), and the more they draw, the more often a correct answer turns up somewhere, so coverage, the fraction of problems with at least one correct try, climbs and appears to be progress. But a deployed system must return one answer, and choosing it, not knowing which try is right, is selection; selection is capped, and past a point extra samples only make the model surer of a confident mistake, even as every draw adds cost. The gap between climbing coverage and stalled selection, the identifiability gap, is the answer a model can produce but not pick. So the real question is not whether to sample but how far, and the answer is: not far. For picking an answer, the vote has already settled within a few dozen draws, the modal ceiling; for scoring a benchmark, sooner still, the correlation ceiling. Beyond that, extra draws cost compute and add nothing, and can even make the answer worse. This paper turns the cutoff into a single number, the effective number of samples, that any sampling run already reveals. The bottleneck is recognizing a right answer, not generating one.

sections.title: Start
sections.content: 6 2 0 2 7 2 ] . [ 1 1 6 6 8 2 . 6 0 6 2 : r When More Sampling Hurts: The Modal Ceiling and Correlation Ceiling of Test-Time Scaling Yong Yi Bay* Kathleen A. Yearick* PhD, University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign T T People overthink; language models over-sample, and the extra effort can talk both into worse answer. Reasoning systems answer hard question by sampling it many times (test-time scaling), and the more they draw, the more often correct answer turns up somewhere, so coverage, the fraction of problems with at least one correct try, climbs and appears to be progress. But deployed system must return one answer, and choosing it, not knowing which try is right, is selection; selection is capped, and past point extra samples only make the model surer of confident mistake, even as every draw adds cost. The gap between climbing coverage and stalled selection, the identifiability gap, is the answer model can produce but not pick. So the real question is not whether to sample but how far, and the answer is: not far. For picking an answer, the vote has already settled within few dozen draws, the modal ceiling; for scoring benchmark, sooner still, the correlation ceiling. Beyond that, extra draws cost compute and add nothing, and can even make the answer worse. This paper turns the cutoff into single number, the effective number of samples, that any sampling run already reveals. The bottleneck is recognizing right answer, not generating one. Keywords test-time scaling inference-time compute repeated sampling pass@k coverage best-of-n self-consistency effective sample size design effect intraclass correlation correlation ceiling modal ceiling identifiability gap

sections.title: 1 Introduction and roadmap
sections.content: A reasoning model can be made stronger at inference time by spending more compute, and the compute goes to one of two levers: longer reasoning, letting the model think more before it answers, or more sampling, drawing answers to one prompt and combining them [14]. This is test-time scaling, and it drives much of the progress in reasoning systems [58]; the sampling lever is the subject here. Its headline curve is coverage, the fraction of problems on which at least one of samples is correct [9], and it climbs over several orders of magnitude of [2], reading as steadily rising capability. That reading is too generous. deployed system must return single answer, and with nothing to certify which sample is right it can only select one, by frequency or learned score; selection is capped, and more sampling does not lift it and can even lower it. correct answer thus grows ever easier to reach but no easier to return: the bottleneck in test-time scaling is not generating right answer, it is recognizing one. And because each draw costs compute, the question that matters is how far to sample, which the ceilings below answer with small, goal-dependent budget (Figure 1). *Equal contribution. Correspondence: {yongyibay, kallie.a.yearick}@gmail.com. 1 Figure 1: The two ceilings of test-time sampling. (a) Coverage, self-consistency, and the identifiability gap between them. (b) The effective number of samples neff against the nominal count n, with the correlation ceiling 1/ρ. The split. Coverage and selection ask different questions of the same samples, and only one of them keeps improving. Coverage asks whether any sample is correct; verifier that could spot the correct one would let coverage ride past every limit toward what the model can ever reach. Selection must commit to one answer with no such verifier, and plurality vote converges to the models most common answer; on the problems where that answer is wrong, more samples only make the wrong answer win more surely, so selection saturates at the fraction of problems whose most common answer is correct, and can even decline as the budget grows. Brown et al. [2] report exactly this pattern without mechanism, that majority voting and reward models plateau beyond several hundred samples and fail to fully scale with the sample budget. The distance between rising coverage and stalled selection is the set of problems the model can solve but cannot pick out, the identifiability gap: it can generate the right answer without being able to select it (Figure 2). Figure 2: Generated but not selected. On hard problem the correct answer appears among the sampled attempts, so coverage finds it; but it is not the most common answer, so the plurality vote returns confident wrong one. 2 second, separate ceiling. Even the benchmark accuracy that sampling is meant to estimate is worth less than the sample count suggests, for different reason. The attempts to problem are not independent tries; they are cluster drawn from one prompt, alike the way several people from one household are alike in survey. The classical correction is the design effect deff = 1 + (n 1)ρ of Kish [10], where ρ is the intraclass correlation among the attempts, so the correlated draws are worth only n/deff independent ones [11]. Read for test-time sampling, this is the effective number of samples neff = 1 + (n 1)ρ , neff 1 ρ as , (1) which saturates at hard correlation ceiling 1/ρ: no budget makes correlated attempts worth more than 1/ρ independent ones (Section 2.1). This ceiling governs estimation, not selection; the two are kept apart throughout. The identifiability gap is measured in Section 4.3. What is and is not new. The design effect and effective sample size are not new here; they are Kish [10] and Cochran [11], and the correction of Condorcets theorem for correlated voters is older still [12, 13]. Three recent works apply the same instrument to language-model outputs, but to different objects: Kohli [14] measure the effective votes of panel of distinct judge models in evaluation, Goel et al. [15] quantify error correlation across models, and Nitarach [16], in competition report, track the effective sample size of majority voting across mixed models. None treats single-model test-time scaling as cluster sampling, separates the estimation ceiling from distinct selection ceiling, derives the modal-answer ceiling and its anti-scaling, or connects to the survey-sampling literature. On the coverage side, Schaeffer et al. [17] and Kazdan et al. [18] explain the power-law shape of coverage by heavy-tailed distribution of per-problem difficulty, and Levi [19] reaches power law through memorization ansatz; all assume attempts are conditionally independent given the problem, the within-problem ρw = 0 case of the model below, from which the analysis here recovers power law of the same form. The contribution here is the lens that separates the three quantities nominal sample count confounds: between-problem difficulty spread ρb that caps benchmark estimation and shapes coverage, within-problem dependence measured to be near zero, and the concentration of the answer distribution that caps selection at modal-hit rate πmode and makes it anti-scale, all grounded on released logs. This work is citable derivation, not new algorithm. Section 2 sets up test-time sampling as cluster sampling and fences the scope of the exact claims. Section 3 derives the effective number of samples, the correlation ceiling, and the marginal value of sample. Section 4 separates coverage from selection, explains the identifiability gap between them, and measures it on both an independent-draw log [2] and dependent-draw log [20]. Section 5 decomposes withinand between-problem correlation, gives the compute-allocation rule, an estimator for ρ, and summary table.

sections.title: 2 Test-time sampling is cluster sampling
sections.content: The entire argument rests on one reframing: problem and its repeated attempts form cluster, not fresh draw each time. This section makes the correspondence precise and fences exactly where the claims that follow are exact. Fix prompt and draw responses o1, . . . , on from one model at fixed decoding configuration (the same sampling settings each time). verifier (an automatic checker that marks each answer right or wrong) scores each, giving binary success indicators Yi = 1{ oi is correct for } {0, 1}, = 1, . . . , n. (2) Write = P[Yi = 1] for the per-attempt success probability and = i=1 Yi for the number correct. The three 3 headline quantities of test-time scaling are functions of (Y1, . . . ,Yn): pass@n = P[K 1] , (cid:123)(cid:122) (cid:125) (cid:124) coverage , ˆp = K/n (cid:124) (cid:123)(cid:122) (cid:125) success fraction . 1{K > n/2} (cid:123)(cid:122) (cid:125) (cid:124) majority vote (3) Best-of-n (drawing answers and returning the one learned reward model scores highest) [21], weighted voting, and self-consistency (returning the answer the samples agree on most often) are all read off the sampled distribution and so are functions of the same draws. The independence baseline assumes Y1, . . . ,Yn are i.i.d. (independent and identically distributed) Bernoulli(s) draws, each coin that lands correct with probability s. Then pass@n = 1 (1 s)n, Var( ˆp) = s(1 s)/n, and, for > 1 2 , the majority vote is correct with probability tending to 1 as [9]. The analysis keeps the marginal and replaces independence with exchangeability. Exchangeable attempts. The attempts to one problem are exchangeable: relabeling them does not change their joint distribution, so only how many succeed matters, not which ones. By de Finettis theorem [22], an infinitely exchangeable binary sequence (one fresh session can in principle extend to arbitrarily many attempts) is mixture of i.i.d. sequences, Yi θ i.i.d. Bernoulli(θ ), θ on [0, 1], (4) for some mixing distribution (the spread of these hidden success rates across problems) with mean E[θ ] = s. In words, the model behaves as if each fresh session first draws hidden success rate θ for the problem, then every attempt in that session is an independent θ -weighted coin: good session lands in strong reasoning basin (large θ ), weak one in poor basin (small θ ). The single number that summarizes the dependence is the intraclass correlation ρ, how strongly two attempts on the same problem move together, and the standard measure for clustered binary data, ρ = Corr(Yi,Yj) = Var(θ ) s(1 s) [0, 1], = j. (5) The representation forces Var(θ ) 0, so ρ 0: exchangeable attempts are non-negatively correlated. Independence is ρ = 0 (a degenerate at s); ρ = 1 is total collapse, where session is all correct or all wrong together. Equation (5) is the bridge from the spread of the latent rate θ to the intraclass correlation that drives the design effect. This ρ is correlation of correctness; the concentration of the answer strings, which governs selection, is separate quantity, introduced in Section 4.2. Figure 3 states the correspondence. sampling frame cluster within-cluster draws correction survey population household members test-time benchmark problem attempts oi deff = 1 + (m1)ρ (cid:55) m/deff deff = 1 + (n1)ρ (cid:55) neff Figure 3: The survey-to-test-time correspondence. Top: survey, where household of members maps through deff = 1 + (m 1)ρ. Bottom: test-time sampling, where problems attempts map through deff = 1 + (n 1)ρ to neff. 4 2.1 Scope of the exact claims canonical claim is only as strong as the assumptions it names, and the main one here is weaker than it looks. The load-bearing assumption is exchangeability: the attempts to problem are taken order-free, which gives them common success rate and common pairwise correlation ρ and makes deff = 1 + (n 1)ρ and the ceiling 1/ρ exact. Little of this rests on the correlation being the same for every pair. The variance identity of Proposition 1 needs only the common rate s: for any pattern of pairwise correlations it holds with ρ read as the mean pairwise correlation ρ = 1 n(n1) i= Corr(Yi,Yj), so deff = 1 + (n 1) ρ and the ceiling is 1/ ρ. Real sampling departs from equicorrelation (attempts that share long prefix are more alike than attempts that branch early); then 1/ρ is simply read as 1/ ρ, the average the estimator of Section 5.3 already returns. What the ceiling truly requires is only that the correlation be positive and not vanish, that is, that sampling diversity stay bounded: the de Finetti mixture forces Var(θ ) 0, hence ρ 0, and infinite exchangeability, the one further assumption, carries every limit below. Equicorrelation is thus convenience that turns an average into single number, not crutch the ceiling stands on. Under these assumptions the design effect in Section 3 is exact for one estimand: the success fraction ˆp = K/n, which depends on (Yi) only through their sum and so estimates the per-problem rate or, pooled, the benchmark mean. It governs how precisely sampling budget pins down accuracy, the estimation use of test-time sampling. Two other quantities read the same draws but are not this mean and are treated on their own terms. Coverage, pass@n = P[K 1], depends on the full mixing distribution G, and Section 4.1 treats it directly through Equation (4). Selection (self-consistency, best-of-n) returns the most-favored answer, the mode of the categorical answer distribution rather than sum of the Yi, and Section 4.2 gives it its own ceiling. The three are kept apart on purpose, because their different dependence on the same draws is the point here, and it is what lets one budget buy different amounts of each. The verifier is assumed accurate; an imperfect verifier adds second, independent ceiling on usable accuracy, one not folded into ρ here.

sections.title: 3 The effective number of samples
sections.content: Everything downstream, the ceiling and the budget rule, follows from single quantity: the variance of the number correct. This section computes it and reads off the effective number of samples. Proposition 1 (Design effect of test-time sampling). Let Y1, . . . ,Yn be exchangeable with P[Yi = 1] = and Corr(Yi,Yj) = ρ for = j. Then the count = Yi has E[K] = ns, Var(K) = ns(1 s) (cid:2) 1 + (n 1)ρ (cid:3), and the success fraction ˆp = K/n has Var( ˆp) = s(1 s)/neff with the effective number of samples neff = 1 + (n 1)ρ . (6) (7) Proof. Linearity gives E[K] = ns. For the variance, Var(K) = Var(Yi) + i= Cov(Yi,Yj). Each Var(Yi) = s(1 s) and each of the n(n 1) covariances equals ρ s(1 s), so Var(K) = ns(1 s) + n(n 1)ρ s(1 s) = ns(1 s)[1 + (n 1)ρ]. Dividing by n2 gives Var( ˆp) = s(1 s)[1 + (n 1)ρ]/n = s(1 s)/neff. The bracket deff = 1 + (n 1)ρ is the design effect: the factor by which correlation inflates the variance of the count relative to independent draws [10, 11]. Equivalently, the covariance of the attempts is constant diagonal plus single rank-one term, Σ = s(1 s)(cid:2)(1 ρ)I + ρ 11(cid:3), so the variance of the count collapses to the scalar deff without ever forming that matrix: the same structured-operator economy that lets large numerical systems be solved matrix-free [23]. The estimator ˆp behaves as if it were built from neff independent samples; Appendix confirms the variance identity (6) against Monte Carlo simulation. 3.1 The correlation ceiling This is the result this work is named for, and its single most important fact: the effective number of samples does not grow without bound. Equation (7) has finite limit. Corollary 1 (Correlation ceiling). If ρ > 0, then neff increases in to the finite limit lim neff = 1 ρ , (8) and neff reaches half of this ceiling at = (1 ρ)/ρ 1/ρ. Proof. Write neff = n/[ 1 ρ + nρ ] 1/ρ as . Setting neff = 1/(2ρ) gives 2ρn = 1 + (n 1)ρ, i.e. ρn = 1 ρ, so = (1 ρ)/ρ. This is the central fact. correlated cluster with intraclass correlation ρ is worth at most 1/ρ independent draws, however large the budget (exactly under the equicorrelation of Section 2.1, and as limiting average otherwise); and it gets halfway there by 1/ρ. Figure 4 plots the ceiling. model with ρ = 0.1 caps out near ten effective samples: the thousandth draw is almost worthless. The ceiling is also why the marginal value of sample collapses. Figure 4: The correlation ceiling. The effective number of samples neff = n/[1 + (n 1)ρ] against the number drawn, for four correlation levels, each saturating at its ceiling 1/ρ (dashed); the independent ideal neff = is the diagonal. Corollary 2 (Value of the n-th sample). The marginal effective sample contributed by the n-th draw is neff dn = 1 ρ (cid:2) 1 + (n 1)ρ (cid:3)2 1 ρ ρ 2 n2 (n ), (9) 6 so the worth of an added sample decays quadratically and is negligible once 1/ρ. The marginal value starts at neff/dn = 1 ρ at = 1 (the second draw already adds only (1 ρ)/(1 + ρ) effective samples) and then falls off as 1/(ρn)2. Figure 5 shows the collapse and marks the break-even point 1/ρ at which the curve has already given up most of its value. This is budget rule, derived in Section 5.2: spending past 1/ρ samples buys redundancy, not signal. worked reading makes the decay concrete. At ρ = 0.1 the first draw is worth full independent sample; by Corollary 2 the tenth is worth (1ρ)/[1+9ρ]2 = 0.9/1.92 0.25, and the hundredth only 0.9/10.92 0.008. thousand-sample run therefore carries the estimation information of roughly its first ten draws, and the rest refine the estimate by almost nothing. The decay is quadratic, so each tenfold increase in budget past the ceiling returns about hundredth as much, which is why the curves of Figure 5 fall to the axis so quickly. Figure 5: The value of the n-th sample, neff/dn from Corollary 2, against the sample index. Break-even points 1/ρ marked dotted (one per ρ).

sections.title: 4 Coverage rises, selection saturates
sections.content: The most-cited puzzle in test-time scaling is that one curve keeps rising while another stalls, and no single mechanism connects them. This section gives the mechanism: coverage and selection read the same samples for different things, so they obey different ceilings. Coverage is capped only by what the model could ever produce; selection by whether its most common answer is correct. The gap between the two is exactly the solvable problems whose correct answer the vote fails to return. Why selection matters at all bears stating, since coverage looks like the whole story. Coverage can be cashed 7 only through verifier that certifies correct answer, and perfect, general verifier is the rare exception rather than the rule. few domains supply one: code against test suite, proof against proof assistant, numeric answer against key. Most do not, and the verifiers that do exist score proxy rather than truth, finite test suite or learned reward model that wrong answer can still pass. Wherever no sound verifier is available, which is the typical deployment, single answer can be returned only by selection, so the modal ceiling of Section 4.2, not coverage, fixes the accuracy system delivers. Coverage measures what the model could reach given verifier it will not have; selection measures what ships. 4.1 Coverage and the correlation tax Coverage is the optimistic half of the split, the curve that keeps climbing; this subsection prices what correlation quietly subtracts from it. Coverage asks only whether some sample is correct, with no need to know which one. Under the mixture (4), pass@n = 1 P[K = 0] = 1 Eθ (cid:2)(1 θ )n(cid:3). (10) Coverage can only rise as the budget grows, and outside hard core of unreachable problems it rises all the way. Proposition 2 (Coverage rises without within-problem ceiling). Under the mixture (4), pass@n is nondecreasing in n, strictly increasing whenever places mass on (0, 1), and converges to 1 π0, where π0 = G({0}) is the fraction of problems the model never solves. For fixed problem with reachability pq(cq) = π > 0, coverage 1 (1 π)n strictly increases to 1. Proof. For each θ [0, 1] the map (cid:55) (1 θ )n is non-increasing, strictly for θ (0, 1). Taking the mixture expectation, P[K = 0] = Eθ [(1 θ )n] is non-increasing in n, strictly if charges (0, 1), so pass@n = 1 P[K = 0] is non-decreasing, strictly so. Since (1 θ )n 1{θ = 0} pointwise, dominated convergence gives P[K = 0] π0, hence pass@n 1 π0. The fixed-problem case is the point mass θ = π. Correlation always taxes coverage relative to the independent reading. Proposition 3 (Correlation tax on coverage). For exchangeable attempts with per-attempt success s, pass@n 1 (1 s)n, (11) with equality if and only if ρ = 0. The gap grows with the dispersion of θ : mean-preserving spread of the difficulty distribution (more very-easy and very-hard problems at the same average) can only widen it. Proof. The map θ (cid:55) (1 θ )n is strictly convex on [0, 1] for 2. By Jensens inequality (the average of convex function is at least the function of the average), Eθ [(1 θ )n] (1 E[θ ])n = (1 s)n, with equality iff θ is degenerate, i.e. ρ = 0. Negating gives Equation (11). So the textbook curve 1 (1 s)n is an upper bound, not prediction: real coverage runs below it whenever samples are correlated. The shape of the shortfall is governed by the lower tail of G, the problems the model rarely solves. Proposition 4 (Power-law coverage from heterogeneity). Let θ Beta(α, β ) (a flexible family of success-rate distributions on the unit interval, standing in for the spread of problem difficulty), so = α/(α + β ) and ρ = 1/(α + β + 1). Then the miss rate is P[K = 0] = B(α, β + n) B(α, β ) Γ(α + β ) Γ(β ) nα (n ), (12) 8 so coverage approaches its limit as power law nα , not the exponential (1 s)n. Proof. With θ Beta(α, β ), E[(1 θ )n] = B(α, β + n)/B(α, β ) is the standard beta-binomial (a binomial Γ(β +n) whose success rate is itself drawn from the Beta) probability of zero successes. Writing it as Γ(α+β ) Γ(α+β +n) Γ(β ) and using Γ(β + n)/Γ(α + β + n) nα (Stirling) gives the tail. The intraclass correlation of beta-binomial is ρ = 1/(α + β + 1). Proposition 4 is the difficulty-heterogeneity story of Schaeffer et al. [17] and Kazdan et al. [18], recovered here as the coverage face of the same model for Beta difficulty prior: heavy lower tail (small α) yields the slowly rising, log-linear coverage that Brown et al. [2] fit empirically, with the exponent set by the lower tail of G. Figure 6 contrasts the exponential and power-law regimes. The overdispersed binomial behind it (a count with more spread than independent draws would give) is classical [24]. If additionally places an atom π0 at θ = 0, hard core of attempts the model never makes correct, then pass@n 1 π0 < 1 and no budget crosses it; this is mode collapse in its starkest form. The atom is ceiling of model capability rather than of sampling: those problems lie outside the models reach, generalization limit that no sampling budget repairs [25]. The practical force of the power law is how slowly it pays. An exponential miss rate (1 s)n clears any target in handful of samples, each one cutting the miss rate by constant factor; power-law miss rate nα does not. Halving an exponential miss rate costs fixed number of further samples; halving power law costs fixed multiplicative factor 21/α , so for heavy lower tail (small α) each successive halving costs as much sampling as everything before it combined. Coverage keeps rising, but with sharply diminishing speed: the slow, log-linear climb that the released logs show over four orders of magnitude. Figure 6 plots both regimes on loglog axes, where the exponential falls off cliff and the power law settles onto straight line of slope α. 9 Figure 6: Exponential versus power-law coverage. The miss rate 1 pass@n on loglog axes: the independent exponential against the power law nα of Proposition 4. Here = 0.5 and ρ {0.1, 0.3}.

sections.title: 4.2 Selection and the modal answer
sections.content: Selection is where the visible gains stop, and the cause is not correlation but the shape of the answer distribution. To return an answer without verifier, method keeps the most common answer (self-consistency [1]) or the highest-scored one (reward-model best-of-n); either way it reads the whole distribution over answer strings, not just the per-attempt correctness rate. Fix problem and let the model induce distribution pq(a) over candidate answers a, with correct answer cq and unique most common answer, the mode = arg maxa pq(a). plurality vote among attempts returns the empirical mode. Proposition 5 (Selection ceiling). As the plurality answer converges almost surely to the mode q, so self-consistency accuracy on fixed problem tends to 1{a = cq}. Averaged over benchmark, self-consistency accuracy converges to the modal-hit rate πmode = Pq (cid:2) = cq (cid:3), (13) hard ceiling, the modal ceiling, with no dependence on the sample budget n. Proof. For fixed problem the empirical answer frequencies converge to pq almost surely (the law of large numbers applied to the multinomial counts), and arg max is continuous wherever the maximizer is unique, so the empirical mode converges to = cq}, and averaging over problems gives the limit (13), which is constant in n. almost surely. Hence 1{plurality correct} 1{a 10 The ceiling is wall, not slowdown: once the budget reveals the mode, more samples cannot move the vote, and on the problems where the mode is wrong they move it the wrong way. Corollary 3 (Anti-scaling of selection). On any problem whose mode is incorrect (a = cq) yet whose correct answer is reachable (pq(cq) > 0), self-consistency accuracy falls to 0 while coverage rises to 1 as : more sampling makes selection worse and coverage better on the same problem. Proof. By Proposition 5 the plurality converges to 1 (1 pq(cq))n 1 because pq(cq) > 0. = cq, so 1{plurality correct} 0, while pass@n = Anti-scaling is the mechanism behind the limited and sometimes non-monotone returns that self-consistency and reward-model selection show in practice [26, 27]: extra samples sharpen confident wrong answer. How fast problem reaches its ceiling is set by the concentration of pq, summarized by the effective number of answers 1/ pq(a)2; the ceiling height πmode is set by whether the mode it converges to happens to be correct. Figure 7 shows both outcomes: coverage rises to one whether or not the mode is correct, while selection rises to one only when the mode is correct and otherwise falls to zero. Self-consistency and plurality voting meet this modal ceiling exactly. Best-of-n with learned reward model is bounded instead by how well that scorer ranks answers: perfect verifier lifts it to coverage, frequency-like scorer collapses it to the modal ceiling, and real reward models fall between. Figure 7: Anti-scaling of selection. Two problems sampled to attempts each: coverage (any attempt correct) and self-consistency (the plurality answer) against n. Left: problem whose most common answer is correct. Right: problem whose most common answer is wrong. The familiar majority-vote reading is the binary special case, and it is strict lower bound. Collapse the answers to two, the correct one against single modal error, and let θ be the latent per-attempt correctness of Equation (4); then plurality is majority, K/n θ almost surely, and the vote is correct in the limit exactly when θ > 1 2 . Corollary 4 (Condorcet jury, correlated). If the difficulty distribution places positive mass on problems 2 ] > 0), majority-vote accuracy converges to P[θ > the model gets wrong more often than right (P[θ < 1 11 2 ] < 1, below the value 1 that independent jurors reach when > 1 1 θ (s, ρ s(1 s)) gives P[θ > 1 P[θ > 1 2 forces every wrong answer below 1 2 ] πmode, because θ > 1 2 ] Φ(cid:0)(s 2 [12, 13]; the normal approximation 2 )/(cid:112)ρ s(1 s)(cid:1). This never exceeds the plurality ceiling, 2 and so below the correct one. The two bounds part company on real logs, and the distance between them is the point. On the dependent-draw log of Section 4.3 the majority bound reads P[θ > 1 2 ] = 0.20 while the plurality ceiling is πmode = 0.45: more than half of the selectable accuracy comes from problems the model answers correctly less than half the time, where the correct answer is still the single most common one because the errors scatter. Figure 8 shows the binary plateau and that simulated accuracy lands on it. Figure 8: The binary (majority-vote) special case of the selection ceiling. Majority-vote accuracy against for several ρ: the independent case (ρ 0, top) and correlated cases plateauing at P[θ > 1 2 ] (Corollary 4), with its normal approximation dashed. Markers: simulation; = 0.65.

sections.title: 4.3 The coverage–selection gap, measured
sections.content: Theory has predicted gap; this is where it is measured on real sampling logs. Put the two together. Coverage (Proposition 3) climbs toward what the model can reach, 1 π0, slowly. Selection (Proposition 5) saturates at the modal-hit rate πmode. The two diverge, and the divergence is the identifiability gap: the problems whose correct answer is in the pool but is not its most common answer. The split shows up in two released logs that probe the two effects separately: an independent-draw log for the between-problem difficulty spread, and dependent-draw log for the within-problem answer collapse. The between-problem term, on independent draws. Brown et al. [2] sampled up to 104 solutions per problem on GSM8K [21] (grade-school math word problems) and MATH [28] (harder competition mathematics) and recorded the correctness of each. Their attempts are drawn independently, so the withinproblem correlation is zero by construction; what the logs expose is the between-problem difficulty spread ρb and its consequences: the ρw = 0 face of the model. The analysis here estimates, per configuration, the 12 difficulty mean s, the intraclass correlation ˆρ = Var(θ )/[s(1 s)], coverage by the unbiased estimator of Chen et al. [9], and self-consistency by plurality vote of the extracted answers (validated against the logs own correctness labels). The self-consistency plateau is reported only where the numeric extractor reproduces those labels; MATH answers are boxed expressions numeric extractor cannot parse, so MATH shows coverage and ˆρ alone. Table 1 reports them. Table 1: The between-problem difficulty correlation ˆρb and the coverageselection gap, from the released logs of Brown et al. [2] (104 samples per problem). The ˆρb 95% CI is problem-level clustered bootstrap (104 resamples). Self-consistency is n/a on MATH (boxed answers numeric extractor cannot parse). Benchmark Model ˆρb ˆρb 95% CI 1/ ˆρb coverage@104 self-cons. GSM8K GSM8K MATH MATH Llama-3-8B-Instruct Llama-3-70B-Instruct Llama-3-8B-Instruct Llama-3-70B-Instruct 0.77 0.93 0.27 0.48 0.47 0.41 0.48 0.61 [0.40, 0.54] [0.24, 0.54] [0.41, 0.55] [0.54, 0.66] 2.1 2.5 2.1 1. 1.00 1.00 0.98 0.98 0.87 0.97 n/a n/a Two readings stand out. First, the difficulty correlation is large and stable, ˆρ 0.40.6 across models and benchmarks, so by Equation (7) the 104 samples drawn for each problem carry the benchmark-mean information of only neff 2 independent samples: for estimating benchmark accuracy, ten thousand samples of one problem are worth about two independent ones. Second, coverage and selection diverge in the direction the split predicts. Figure 9 shows the GSM8K, Llama-3-8B-Instruct curves: coverage reaches 1.00 while self-consistency plateaus at 0.87. For roughly an eighth of problems the correct answer is somewhere in the pool but the vote does not return it even at the plateau: the usable-signal gap, on real data. 13 Figure 9: The coverageselection gap on real logs [2]: GSM8K, Llama-3-8B-Instruct, up to 104 samples per problem. Coverage rises to 1.00; self-consistency plateaus at 0.87; the shaded band is the usable-signal gap. The reading is consistent across the repeated-sampling literature. Chen et al. [26] find voting performance non-monotone in the number of calls because benchmark mixes easy and hard problems, the anti-scaling of Corollary 3; Wang et al. [27] show that prompt-diversity interventions, which spread the answer distribution, are what move best-of-n; and Kirk et al. [29] document that post-training (the fine-tuning applied after pretraining) sharpens the modal answer. Each is face of one split: selection is held at its modal ceiling, coverage is not. The selection ceiling, on raw answers. The modal-hit rate πmode needs the answer strings, which the correctness-only logs above do not record; log of raw completions supplies them. The best-of-n release of Beeching et al. [20] samples each of the 500 MATH-500 problems 256 times from one model (Llama-3.21B-Instruct) at fixed decoding configuration (temperature 0.8, top-p 1.0), recording every raw completion. Across the 500 problems the 256 answers carry median of only about thirteen distinct values (an effective answer count 1/ p2 a), not 256: the answer distribution pq is sharply concentrated. Figure 10 measures the consequence: coverage (any attempt correct) climbs to 0.88 while self-consistency (the plurality answer) plateaus at πmode = 0.45 by about = 64 and moves little after. Correctness is graded with the same verifier that produced the datasets labels (math-verify), reproducing its reported single-sample accuracy ( 0.27) to about point. The binary majority bound on the same log reads only P[θ > 1 2 ] = 0.20: half the realized selection accuracy comes from problems solved less than half the time whose scattered errors leave the correct answer as the single most common one. The plateau is the modal ceiling of Proposition 5, on real attempts: the correct answer reaches the pool for nearly nine problems in ten, but the most common answer is correct for only four and half. 14 Figure 10: The within-problem coverageselection gap on dependent-draw log [20]: MATH-500, Llama-3.2-1BInstruct, 256 attempts per problem in one session (averaged over five sessions). Coverage rises to 0.88; self-consistency plateaus at 0.45; the shaded band is the within-problem usable-signal gap. The five sessions also test the decomposition itself. Estimating ρw as the run-to-run dispersion of problems success rate across the five independent sessions, corrected for the binomial noise of finite session, gives ˆρw near zero ( ˆρw 0.0007, 95% CI [0.0005, 0.0009]): re-running fixed model at fixed temperature does not move the latent success rate beyond sampling noise, so the de Finetti rate θ is nearly fixed per problem and the same-session pooled correlation is dominated by difficulty. The two-stage identity then holds on real data to within 0.001: the directly pooled same-session correlation is ˆρ = 0.401, against ˆρb + (1 ˆρb) ˆρw = 0.402 from the separate terms (Equation (14), with this logs own ˆρb = 0.402). The selection plateau in Figure 10 therefore is not between-session effect: it is the within-session answer collapse, the concentration of the answer distribution pq rather than any drift in the success rate, which Proposition 5 prices as the modal-hit rate πmode and Corollary 4 lower-bounds by P[θ > 1 2 ]. Repeated sampling answers two questions that single accuracy number blurs. Coverage asks whether correct answer is reachable at all, and keeps rising with the budget. Selection asks whether the models most common answer is the correct one, and is capped at the modal-hit rate πmode. Their difference is the identifiability gap: the problems model can reach but not return.

sections.title: 5 Decomposition, allocation, and measurement
sections.content: A lens earns its place only if it can be measured and acted on. This section splits the correlation into its two sources, turns the ceiling into compute-allocation rule, and gives one-line estimator for ρ that runs on any sampling log already in hand. 5.1 Withinand between-problem correlation Two sources feed the pooled correlation ρ, and separating them is what tells benchmark mean from single answer. Within-problem correlation ρw is run-to-run dependence on fixed problem: the dispersion of the latent rate θ across independent sessions in Equation (5). Under standard independent decoding this is zero by construction, each attempt an independent draw given the prompt, and Section 4.3 measures it at ˆρw 0.0007. Between-problem heterogeneity ρb is the spread of difficulty across benchmark: two attempts to randomly drawn problem move together merely because they share that problems difficulty. For attempts pooled over benchmark the variance components add, so the pooled intraclass correlation (taking ρw common across problems) satisfies ρ = ρb + (1 ρb) ρw, (14) the standard two-stage design effect of clustered sampling [11]: the total same-problem correlation is the between-problem difficulty spread plus the within-problem dependence left after it. With ρw 0 the pooled ρ is essentially ρb, so the correlation ceiling of Section 3 is statement about estimating benchmark from heterogeneous problems, not about repeated tries on one problem. The slow, power-law coverage of benchmark is the same ρb through Proposition 4, the regime studied by Schaeffer et al. [17]. The selection ceiling is third axis, the concentration of the answer distribution pq (Section 4.2), which ρ does not see: difficulty heterogeneity, run-to-run dependence, and answer-mode collapse are three distinct quantities the nominal sample count silently confounds.

sections.title: 5.2 A compute-allocation rule
sections.content: This is the practical payoff: with fixed compute budget, the goal decides how to spend it, because the three goals reach their ceilings at different points. Let total inference compute be nL for samples of reasoning length L. The levers are more samples (raise n), longer reasoning (raise L, which raises s), more problems (for evaluation), and decorrelation (spread the answer distribution via temperature, nucleus (top-p) sampling, prompt diversity, or mixing models) [7, 27, 30]. The ceilings of Sections 3 and 4.2 (Corollaries 12 and Proposition 5) set the stopping point for each: To estimate benchmark mean, 1/ρb samples per problem suffice (about two at the measured ρb); spend the rest on more problems. To select an answer without verifier, sampling helps only until the plurality stabilizes, on the order of the effective answer count 1/ pq(a)2, and past that it can anti-scale. To cover, that is to find correct sample for verifier, there is no within-problem ceiling and more samples keep paying. The rule reframes the think longer or sample more question [3, 4, 31]: sampling pays without limit for coverage, stops early for selection, and stops almost at once for estimation, so the budget should follow the goal rather than single number. Whether decorrelation actively raises the selection plateau is the lever the protocol below tests. 16 5.3 Estimating ρ The ceiling is actionable only if ρ can be read off runs already in hand, and it can. The correlation is measurable from any sampling log that records, for each of problems, the number correct ci out of ni attempts. The standard moment (analysis-of-variance) estimator for clustered binary data [11] compares the betweenand within-problem sums of squares of ˆpi = ci/ni, ˆρ = MSbetween MSwithin MSbetween + (n0 1) MSwithin , (15) with n0 the average cluster size; equivalently, the beta-binomial model of Kazdan et al. [18] has intraclass correlation ˆρ = 1/( ˆα + ˆβ + 1) by the identity in Appendix A. Two cautions. On benchmarks with many fully-solved or never-solved problems the within-problem variance is degenerate and Equation (15) can return small or negative ˆρ, which should be clipped at zero; and pooled ˆρ mixes ρb and ρw, so it states how much of the nominal is real only at the level (a single problem or whole benchmark) at which it was estimated. Table 1 runs the estimator on the logs of Brown et al. [2]; because those attempts are drawn independently, what it recovers is the between-problem term ρb, with clustered (problem-level) bootstrap interval that resamples problems with replacement. The within-problem term needs dependent draws, which Section 4.3 reads off the single-session log of Beeching et al. [20]: the seed-to-seed spread of problems success rate is negligible there ( ˆρw 0.0007), so re-decoding one prompt barely moves the latent rate, yet within session the answer distribution still narrows to about thirteen modes and self-consistency plateaus at 0.45. The single-model selection ceiling is therefore set not by run-to-run drift but by the concentration of the answer distribution, the modal-hit rate πmode of Proposition 5. Genuine correlation does cap voting once the voters are distinct models rather than repeated draws of one: across nine distinct judge models the mean pairwise error correlation is ˆρ 0.39, so the nine are worth only about two effective votes [14]. For repeated draws of single model, by contrast, competition report finds high-temperature sampling already largely decorrelates the errors [16], so what holds selection down there is not run-to-run correlation but the concentration of the answer mode: the handful of effective answers that makes selection saturate so early. The practical recommendation is one line, meant to be copied into methods section verbatim: Estimate ˆρ from the sampling log via Equation (15), then report the effective number of samples neff = n/[1 + (n 1) ˆρ] alongside the nominal count n, with the ceiling 1/ ˆρ. This is the design-effect-corrected count of Kish [10], and it lets reader see how much of sampling budget is real. pre-registered protocol for the decoding lever. Section 4.3 measures the within-problem ceiling at one decoding configuration: the latent run rate θ is nearly fixed per problem ( ˆρw 0), so the selection plateau is set by the dispersion of the modal answer within session, not by run-to-run drift. What remains to be measured is the lever: whether the decoding choices that decorrelate answers raise the plateau, the prediction the allocation rule rests on. The following test is pre-registered. Fix benchmark and model. For each of 200 problems and each of several decoding configurations (a sweep over temperature, nucleus p, and prompt diversity), draw 256 verified attempts; measure the within-session answer-effective count 1/ p2 a, the plurality plateau, and the answer-indicator intraclass correlation. The pre-registered prediction, from Proposition 5, is that configurations with lower answer correlation have higher plurality plateau, monotone in the effective answer count; the falsifiable alternative is that the plateau is flat in the decoding configuration, under which decorrelation would not be the lever the rule names. This requires model inference and is left to follow-on work; it is stated now so the decoding lever is testable rather than assumed. 17 5.4 practitioners reference Everything needed to apply the lens fits on page: short checklist of what to do, table of the formulas, and reading of the common methods. The checklist comes first, and these are diagnostics, not universal defaults. 1. For evaluation, report neff, not just n: for the benchmark mean budget of per problem is worth n/[1 + (n 1)ρb] independent observations, at most 1/ρb, so add problems rather than samples. 2. Estimate ρb from the log with Equation (15), clipped at zero on saturated benchmarks. 3. Distinguish the three goals: coverage keeps improving with n, selection plateaus at πmode and can anti-scale, estimation saturates by 1/ρb. 4. For selection, lower the answer concentration (temperature, nucleus sampling, prompt diversity, model mixing) rather than raising n; sampling past the effective answer count buys nothing. 5. Separate the answer ceiling from the verifier ceiling: one is whether the correct answer is the mode, the other whether the right try is identifiable by the scorer. concrete pass through the checklist fixes the workflow. Take benchmark on which the difficulty correlation is ˆρb 0.5 and the answers concentrate onto about dozen modes per problem, the regime of the logs in Section 4.3. For evaluation, budget of 256 samples per problem is worth only neff 1/ρb 2 independent observations of the benchmark mean, so confidence interval computed as if the 256 were independent is too narrow by roughly elevenfold ((cid:112)256/2); the honest move is to report neff and to spend fresh compute on fresh problems. For deployment with verifier, the same 256 samples keep paying, since coverage carries no within-problem ceiling. For deployment without one, self-consistency has captured almost all it will within few dozen samples, small multiple of the effective answer count, and drawing the full 256 risks anti-scaling on the problems whose mode is wrong. Three numbers drive the three goals: the difficulty correlation ρb, the effective answer count, and the verifiers accuracy. The first two are read off any sampling log already in hand by the estimators above, and the third is property of the scorer. Reading them once states how much of budget is real for each use, which is the whole of what the lens asks of practitioner. Table 2 collects the formulas behind the checklist, each with the behavior it explains, from the design effect that opens the argument to the modal-hit rate that closes it. Table 2: Formulas for correlated test-time scaling. Here is the number of samples, the per-attempt success probability, and ρ the intraclass correlation of the success indicators. Quantity Design effect Formula deff = 1 + (n 1)ρ Effective number of samples neff = n/[1 + (n 1)ρ] Correlation ceiling neff 1/ρ Value of the n-th sample (1 ρ)/[1 + (n 1)ρ]2 Coverage tax Coverage tail pass@n 1 (1 s)n P[K=0] Γ(α+β ) Γ(β ) nα Selection ceiling πmode = Pq[ q correct ] Effective number of answers 1/ pq(a)2 Majority-vote bound P[θ > 1 2 ] Φ(cid:0) s1/2 ρs(1s) Interpretation Correlation inflates the variance of the count. The usable count of independent draws. Sampling cannot exceed 1/ρ effective draws. Marginal worth 1/(ρn)2. decays as Correlation runs coverage below the independent curve. Heterogeneity gives power-law, not exponential, coverage. Self-consistency cannot beat the modal-hit rate. Sets how fast plurality reaches its ceiling. Lower bound on πmode, when errors scatter. loose (cid:1) The formulas price each behavior; reading the methods as estimands then assigns each its ceiling. Coverage escapes every ceiling because it asks only whether correct sample exists; self-consistency and best-of-n meet the modal-answer wall because they read the answer distribution; and only benchmark-mean estimation meets the correlation ceiling, the one place the design effect of Section 3 truly binds. Table 3: Test-time-scaling methods as estimands, with the ceiling that binds each. Method Reads Estimand Ceiling Coverage / pass@n (with verifier) Self-consistency / plurality Best-of-n (reward model) Benchmark-mean estimate Single sample / greedy

sections.title: 6 Conclusion
sections.content: existence indicator any correct sample most common answer mode of pq top-scored sample success fraction ˆp one answer single draw sample mean argmax of reward reachability, no 1/ρ πmode reward-model ranking neff 1/ρb n/a More sampling makes coverage climb while selection stalls; what separates them is the identifiability gap, the solvable problems whose answer vote never returns. Selection stalls because it meets the modal ceiling πmode, fixed by how often the most common answer happens to be right: on the dependent-draw log of Beeching et al. [20] the 256 tries per problem reduce to about thirteen, coverage reaches 0.88 while selection is right for only 0.45, and drawing more only sharpens confident error. different use meets different limit: estimating benchmark mean is capped by the correlation ceiling 1/ρb, so with ˆρb 0.40.6 on 19 the released logs of Brown et al. [2], ten thousand attempts on single problem buy the precision of about two, while the seed-to-seed term stays near zero ( ˆρw 0.0007), leaving coverage with no within-problem ceiling. One sample count thus stands in for three different things at once: difficulty spread between problems, dependence between runs, and the collapse of answers onto mode. When to stop. Because each draw costs compute and both ceilings are low, the budget that pays is small and set by the goal: about 1/ρb samples to estimate benchmark mean, on the order of the effective number of answers to select one, and no limit for coverage where verifier can pick the correct sample out. Reporting the effective number of samples beside the nominal n, closed form solved for rather than searched [32], says how much of budget actually counts, and where it stops paying. The bottleneck in test-time scaling has moved from generating correct answer to recognizing one: coverage shows the answers are already present, the modal ceiling shows that more sampling will not surface them, and the compute that extra draws cannot use is better spent correlating answers less and choosing among them better, not drawing more.

Solve for the hyperparameter, skip the search: Kolmogorovoptimal scaling laws for spline regression. arXiv preprint arXiv:2606.23575, 2026. 22 Appendix A: Elementary derivations The body states the propositions and leans on these short calculations without pausing for them; each one underwrites result above. Beta-binomial moments and intraclass correlation. Take the latent rate θ Beta(α, β ) with attempts Yi θ i.i.d. Bernoulli(θ ). The Beta has mean and variance = E[θ ] = α α + β , Var(θ ) = αβ (α + β )2(α + β + 1) = s(1 s) α + β + 1 . Two distinct attempts share only the rate θ , so their covariance is the variance of that shared rate: for = j, Cov(Yi,Yj) = E[θ 2] s2 = Var(θ ). Dividing by s(1 s) gives the intraclass correlation of Equation (5), ρ = Var(θ ) s(1 s) = 1 α + β + 1 , which does not depend on n. Inverting, target mean and correlation ρ are realized by α = s(1 ρ)/ρ and β = (1 s)(1 ρ)/ρ, the map the figures use to set Beta from (s, ρ). Zero-success probability and its tail. The chance that none of attempts succeeds is the Beta average of (1 θ )n, standard beta integral: P[K = 0] = Eθ (cid:2)(1 θ )n(cid:3) = (cid:90) 1 0 (1 θ )n θ α1(1 θ )β 1 B(α, β ) dθ = B(α, β + n) B(α, β ) . Written with gamma functions this is Γ(α+β ) Γ(β ) Γ(β + n)/Γ(α + β + n) nα (Stirling, Γ(x + a)/Γ(x + b) xab), so Γ(β +n) Γ(α+β +n) . For large the gamma ratio decays polynomially, P[K = 0] Γ(α + β ) Γ(β ) nα (n ). This is the power-law tail of Proposition 4: coverage approaches its limit polynomially, not exponentially. The selection plateau. Plurality returns the most frequent answer, and the answer counts over attempts are Multinomial(n, pq). By the law of large numbers the empirical frequencies converge to pq almost surely, so the empirical mode converges to the true mode = arg maxa pq(a), and per-problem plurality accuracy to 1{a = cq}. Averaging over problems gives the modal-hit rate πmode of Equation (13). The two-answer case recovers the majority vote. With only correct answer and one error, write θ for the per-attempt correctness; then plurality is majority and ˆp = K/n θ almost surely, so in the limit the majority is right precisely if θ > 1 2 . Its variance tends to Var( ˆp) ρ s(1 s), and approximating θ as (cid:0)s, ρ s(1 s)(cid:1) gives the closed form (cid:32) P[θ > 1 2 ] Φ (cid:33) 1 2 (cid:112)ρ s(1 s) of Corollary 4. Because θ > 1 bound never exceeds πmode. 2 pushes every rival answer under 1 2 , hence under the correct one, this majority 23 Two-stage design effect. Pool attempts over problems, and let µi be the success rate of problem i, with between-problem correlation ρb = Var(µi)/[s(1 s)] and common within-problem correlation ρw given the problem. By the law of total covariance, two same-problem attempts = have Cov(Yi j,Yi j) = E(cid:2)ρw µi(1 µi)(cid:3) + Var(µi). Since E[µi(1 µi)] = s(1 s) Var(µi) = s(1 s)(1 ρb), the right-hand side is s(1 s)(cid:2)ρb + (1 ρb)ρw Dividing by s(1 s) gives the pooled correlation (cid:3). of Equation (14), and the same-problem design effect is 1 + (n 1)ρ [11]. ρ = ρb + (1 ρb)ρw Appendix B: Reproducibility Every number, table, and figure in this work regenerates from clean checkout. The propositions are checked numerically by scripts/verify_math.py, which verifies the identities of Sections 35 (including the variance identity (6), the coverage monotonicity of Proposition 2, the modal-answer selection ceiling (13) with its anti-scaling corollary and the majority-vote lower bound, and the two-stage decomposition (14)) against Monte Carlo simulation; all checks pass. The five model-based figures are generated by scripts/make_figures.py from closed forms and fixed-seed simulation; the correlated attempts use the de Finetti representation θ Beta(α, β ), Yi θ Bernoulli(θ ), with (α, β ) set from (s, ρ) by Appendix A. The between-problem figure and Table 1 are produced by scripts/analyze_brown.py, which downloads the public sampling logs of Brown et al. [2], estimates ˆρb with clustered (problem-level) bootstrap 95% interval (104 resamples, fixed seed), computes the coverage and self-consistency curves, and writes small summary that the figure script reads. The within-problem figure and the ˆρw, ˆρb, and decomposition numbers of Section 4.3 are produced by scripts/analyze_rhow.py, which downloads the five-session best-of-n log of Beeching et al. [20], grades every completion with math-verify (the verifier behind the datasets own labels, whose reported single-sample accuracy it reproduces to about point), estimates the betweenand within-problem correlations with problem-clustered bootstrap intervals, and computes the within-session coverage and plurality curves; the graded per-problem counts are cached so the figure rebuilds without re-downloading or re-grading the multi-hundred-megabyte log. The Python environment is pinned in pyproject.toml (uv sync); make all regenerates everything. Seeds are fixed, so the results are reproducible. The code, the cached result summaries, and the manuscript source are available at https://github.com/bay-yearick-lab/sampling-ceilings.
